name: North Yemen Civil War
summary: The North Yemen Civil War was fought from 1962 to 1970 between republicans of the Yemen Arab Republic and royalists loyal to the Mutawakkilite imamate. It became a major Arab Cold War proxy war, with Egypt/UAR intervening for the republicans and Saudi Arabia, Jordan, British-linked covert networks, and foreign mercenaries supporting the royalists. Overall death estimates are highly contested, with broad historical estimates of 100,000-200,000 killed but much lower strict battle-death coding in PRIO-style datasets.

military_killed.notes: Best-cited side-specific figure is up to 26,000 Egyptian troops killed. This excludes unquantified Yemeni republican combatant deaths. Earlier official Egyptian army figures reported 15,194 dead, but later summaries commonly use 26,000.

breakdown.notes: Reported first gas attack of the war on 8 June 1963, killing about 7 and injuring about 25.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians in a late-1966 gas-bomb attack
breakdown.notes: Reported 11 December 1966 gas-bomb attack with 15 bombs, killing 2 and injuring 35.
ICRC annual-report excerpt says about 120 persons, many women and children, were killed in the 5 January 1967 air raid; other summaries report 140 fatalities and 270 casualties.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians in Gahar/Gadafa or nearby northern village
breakdown.notes: ICRC reported a further May 1967 raid causing 75 deaths; Red Cross medical reporting described survivors' allegations that many inhabitants died of asphyxiation.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The 204-224 range is only the sum of named, documented Egyptian or Egyptian-run republican gas/air-raid incidents found in accessible sources. It is not a total civilian death toll; approximately 40 chemical-weapons incidents were reportedly recorded, but no reliable aggregate death toll was found.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Egyptian bombing and poison-gas attacks have been described as part of a scorched-earth or countryside-depopulation approach, but no defensible numeric estimate of indirect deaths was found.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Civilians displaced or deprived by war conditions
deaths_from_actions.notes: The war produced hunger, hardship, and dislocation; no source found separates disease or deprivation deaths caused by republican/Egyptian actions from the war's combined effects and drought.

military_killed.notes: A historical account reports an estimated 40,000 royalist dead by the mid-war stalemate period. Some summaries separately list 1,000 Saudi dead, but that figure is less well cross-checked and is not included in the numeric low here.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: No credible quantified estimate was found for civilians directly killed by royalist forces. Royalist forces conducted sieges, shelling, and attacks, including the Seventy Day Siege of Sanaa, but civilian fatalities are not separated from combatant deaths in accessible sources.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Civilians in and around republican-held Sanaa and other contested areas
deaths_from_actions.notes: Royalist siege warfare likely contributed to hunger and deprivation, especially during the Seventy Day Siege of Sanaa, but no reliable numeric allocation of indirect civilian deaths to royalist actions was found.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Civilians displaced by prolonged royalist-republican fighting
deaths_from_actions.notes: The war and a long drought together caused hunger, economic hardship, social dislocation, and many deaths, but sources do not allow side-specific quantification.

totals.notes: Military_low adds the best-cited side-specific floor of 26,000 Egyptian military deaths and 40,000 royalist deaths, excluding unquantified Yemeni republican deaths, possible Saudi deaths, and later/final royalist losses. Civilian_low is only a floor from named Egyptian gas/air attacks. Grand_low and grand_high use broad historical all-deaths estimates of 100,000-200,000; PRIO-style battle-death coding cited by RAND gives a much lower 1,200-11,000 and should not be treated as equivalent to total war deaths.

key_atrocities.name: Kitaf/Ketaf air or gas attack
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Republican coalition / Egyptian or Egyptian-run air forces
key_atrocities.notes: ICRC reported about 120 killed in the 5 January 1967 air raid; other summaries give 140 fatalities and 270 casualties.

key_atrocities.name: Gahar/Gadafa / Wadi Hirran gas attack
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Republican coalition / Egyptian or Egyptian-run air forces
key_atrocities.notes: ICRC reported a further raid in May 1967 causing 75 deaths; Red Cross medical reporting described allegations that many inhabitants died of asphyxiation.

key_atrocities.name: Kawma gas attack
key_atrocities.notes: Reported first gas attack of the war on 8 June 1963, killing about 7 people and injuring about 25.

key_atrocities.name: Late-1966 gas-bomb attack
key_atrocities.notes: Reported 11 December 1966 attack in which 15 gas bombs killed 2 and injured 35.

key_atrocities.name: Seventy Day Siege of Sanaa
key_atrocities.victims_group: Republican defenders and civilians in Sanaa
key_atrocities.notes: The siege lasted into February 1968 and was the turning point of the war; no reliable civilian death toll was found.

uncertainty_notes: The North Yemen Civil War lacks a comprehensive casualty registry or truth-commission-style accounting. The strongest side-specific figures are Egyptian military deaths and an estimated royalist combatant toll, but Yemeni republican combatant deaths, civilians killed by conventional fire, and indirect deaths from hunger, disease, drought, and displacement are not reliably disaggregated. Broad all-deaths estimates of 100,000-200,000 coexist with much lower PRIO-style battle-death estimates, reflecting different definitions and likely undercounting.
